did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning model named D3DC-ResU-NeXt++ (combining deformable 3D convolution and ResU-NeXt++) for skin lesion segmentation in dermoscopy images. The method involves CNNs, transfer learning, and the RAdam optimizer, evaluated on the ISIC2018 Task I dataset to achieve a Jaccard index of 86.84%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a new skin lesion segmentation model (D3DC-ResU-NeXt++) with original experimental results, including hyperparameter settings, dataset usage (ISIC2018 Task I), and performance metrics (Jaccard index of 86.84%), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Segmentation of dermoscopy images based on deformable 3D convolution and ResU-NeXt + +
brief_summary: This study proposes D3DC-ResU-NeXt++, a skin lesion segmentation model for dermoscopy images that integrates deformable 3D convolution with ResU-NeXt++ to address parameter redundancy and gradient disappearance issues in traditional CNNs. The authors introduce a novel DCRH (dilation, crop, resize, and hair removal) preprocessing method and utilize the RAdam optimizer to enhance training efficiency and segmentation accuracy. The model was evaluated on the ISIC2018 Task I dataset, which included 2594 images of nevus, seborrheic keratosis, and melanoma. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed method achieved a Jaccard index of 86.84%, outperforming existing models like U-Net++ and ResUNet++. The findings suggest that this approach effectively assists dermatologists in diagnosing skin lesions and determining lesion boundaries.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the ISIC2018 Task I dataset used for segmentation experiments. The paper highlights melanoma as the most dangerous skin cancer and focuses on improving segmentation accuracy for it.
One of the three classes in the ISIC2018 Task I dataset used for segmentation experiments.

all_task_types.target: Binary masks for skin lesion regions in dermoscopy images
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes D3DC-ResU-NeXt++ for skin lesion segmentation. The model predicts pixel-wise binary masks (lesion vs. non-lesion) to determine the boundary between lesions and normal skin. Performance is evaluated using the Jaccard index (86.84%), Dice coefficient, accuracy, and recall on the ISIC2018 Task I dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2018 Task I
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset containing skin lesion images with annotated ground truth class labels. It includes three classes of skin lesion types: nevus, seborrheic keratosis, and melanoma.

use.training: Used for training the D3DC-ResU-NeXt++ model. Specifically, 2044 images were used, with 20% (408 images) held out for validation.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (408 images from the training set). The paper also mentions the dataset originally provided a validation dataset with 100 images, but the experimental setup used 20% of the training set for validation.
use.testing: Used for testing the final model performance (550 images). The paper also notes the dataset originally provided a test dataset with 1000 images, but the study used 550 images for testing.
use.other: The dataset was also used for comparative experiments with other models (e.g., FCN, U-Net++, ResUNet++) and ablation studies on preprocessing (DCRH) and optimizers (RAdam).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models were based on direct metric values (Jaccard index, Dice coefficient, Accuracy, Recall) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC2018 Task I.evaluation_setting: Extensive experiments comparing different segmentation models with RAdam optimizer, DCRH preprocessing, and transfer learning
ISIC2018 Task I.notes: The proposed D3DC-ResU-NeXt++ model achieved the highest performance among the compared models. Compared to Res-UNet, JI was 3.3% higher, DC was 1.28% higher, ACC was 1% higher, and RE was 3% higher. The model converged at the 20th epoch with stable Jaccard index and loss curves.

ISIC2018 Task I (Ablation Study - Compon.evaluation_setting: Comparison of segmentation performance with different combinations of DCRH, RAdam, and Transfer Learning (TL). C03 denotes no components, C33 denotes all components, Avg denotes average of 6 combinations.
ISIC2018 Task I (Ablation Study - Compon.notes: C33 (using RAdam, DCRH, and transfer learning) resulted in JI and DC values 3-4% higher than C03. JI and DC of Avg were 1-2% higher than C03.

ISIC2018 Task I (Ablation Study - Optimi.evaluation_setting: Comparison of optimizers (RMSprop, Adam, NAdam, SGD, RAdam) combined with ResUNet++
ISIC2018 Task I (Ablation Study - Optimi.notes: RAdam achieved the best segmentation results (JI and DC) among the optimizers tested. SGD ranked second in results but took the longest time. RMSprop consumed the shortest time but had poor final results.

ISIC2018 Task I (Ablation Study - Normal.evaluation_setting: Comparison of normalization modules (None, IN, BN, IBN) combined with ResUNet++
ISIC2018 Task I (Ablation Study - Normal.notes: Using the IBN module significantly shortened training time and yielded higher JI and DC compared to other models.

PH2 Dataset.evaluation_setting: Extensive experiments with DCRH, RAdam, and transfer learning combined with different models
PH2 Dataset.notes: DCRH, RAdam, and transfer learning worked well on this dataset with different models, achieving high JI and DC.

ISIC2018 Task I (Sample Expansion Study).notes: The paper discusses testing results on expanded sample datasets but does not provide specific metric values for these expanded sets in the provided text snippets, only noting that the framework has good adaptability.

Comparison with Other Detectors (ISIC201.evaluation_setting: Comparison of JI with other state-of-the-art detectors/segmentors
Comparison with Other Detectors (ISIC201.notes: The proposed model achieved a JI of 0.8684, outperforming the listed comparative models.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from pretrained weights on the Data Science Bowl-2018 dataset for convolutional layers; random initializer for fully connected layers.

layer_details.normalization: IBN (Instance-Batch Normalization) used to increase generalization; IN and BN layers added in lower layers; IN layer removed from original structure in improved dynamic convolution layer.

training_details.hardware: i9-9900k processor, 64 GB memory, GTX2080Ti graphics card

preprocessing_techniques: DCRH (dilation, crop, resize, and hair removal) preprocessing method
Dilation of lesions inside segmentation masks by a factor of 1.4 along each image dimension
Cropping of skin lesion images using dilated lesion masks
Resizing of resulting images to 448 × 448 pixels
Hair removal using morphological operations and black top-hat filter
Inpainting algorithm applied to replace pixel values with neighboring pixels after hair removal
Transfer learning from pretrained weights on the Data Science Bowl-2018 dataset for convolutional layers
Random initializer for fully connected layers

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: DCRH Preprocessing (Dilation, Crop, Resize, Hair Removal)
data_augmentation_techniques.details: A novel data preprocessing method used to enhance the dataset. Lesions inside segmentation masks were dilated by a factor of 1.4 along each image dimension. Dilated lesion masks were used for cropping skin lesion images. Resulting images were resized to 448 × 448 pixels. Hair removal was performed using morphological operations and a black top-hat filter, followed by an inpainting algorithm to replace pixel values with neighboring pixels.

Used as a form of data augmentation/preprocessing by initializing the weights of the convolutional layers with pretrained parameters from the Data Science Bowl-2018 dataset. Fully connected layers were initialized using a random initializer.

The paper conducted experiments on expanded sample datasets to test adaptability. Dataset 1 consisted of 6132 training images and 1650 testing images. Dataset 2 consisted of 4088 training images and 1100 testing images. 20% of the training images in each dataset were used for validation.

conclusions: The proposed D3DC-ResU-NeXt++ model, which integrates deformable 3D convolution with ResU-NeXt++, effectively addresses parameter redundancy and gradient disappearance issues common in traditional 2D CNNs for skin lesion segmentation.
The introduction of the DCRH (dilation, crop, resize, and hair removal) preprocessing method significantly improves the Jaccard index of skin lesion image segmentation.

The use of the RAdam optimizer allows the model to converge quickly without falling into local optimal solutions, outperforming other optimizers like RMSprop, Adam, NAdam, and SGD in both segmentation accuracy and convergence efficiency.
The D3DC-ResU-NeXt++ model achieved a Jaccard index of 86.84% on the ISIC2018 Task I dataset, outperforming existing models such as U-Net++, ResUNet++, FCN, R-CNN, and Mask R-CNN.
Compared to Res-UNet, the proposed model improved the Jaccard index by 3.3%, Dice coefficient by 1.28%, accuracy by 1%, and recall by 3%.

The combination of RAdam, DCRH preprocessing, and transfer learning (C33) resulted in Jaccard index and Dice coefficient values 3-4% higher than when these components were not used (C03), demonstrating their strong applicability across different neural networks.
The IBN (Instance-Batch Normalization) module significantly shortened training time while yielding higher Jaccard index and Dice coefficient compared to other normalization modules (None, IN, BN).
The model demonstrated good adaptability and high segmentation performance on expanded sample datasets (Dataset 1 and Dataset 2) and other datasets like PH2 and ISIC2017.

The proposed method can assist dermatologists in determining and diagnosing the types of skin lesions and the boundary between lesions and normal skin, potentially improving the survival rate of skin cancer patients.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC2018 Task I dataset for primary evaluation, with limited external validation on PH2 and ISIC2017 datasets; results on these external datasets were reported as identical to ISIC2018 in the provided text, suggesting potential data leakage or reporting errors.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for performance comparisons between models, relying solely on direct metric values (Jaccard index, Dice coefficient).

The dataset split described in the text (two-thirds training, one-third testing) conflicts with the standard ISIC2018 Task I split (2044 training, 550 testing) and the previously extracted data counts, indicating potential inconsistency in dataset usage description.

The model's generalizability is limited by the use of transfer learning from the Data Science Bowl-2018 dataset, which may introduce bias if the source domain distribution differs significantly from clinical dermoscopy images.

The preprocessing method DCRH (dilation, crop, resize, hair removal) is dataset-specific and tailored to the ISIC2018 characteristics; its effectiveness may not generalize to datasets with different artifact profiles or lesion sizes without re-tuning.

The paper acknowledges technical challenges such as overlapping skin lesions and varying stages of skin cancer, which are not fully addressed in the current model architecture, limiting clinical robustness.
No ablation study or analysis is provided for the impact of hair removal quality on segmentation accuracy, despite hair being a significant confounder in dermoscopy images.

The hardware specifications (GTX2080Ti) and training times (e.g., 22.5 hours for RAdam) are reported, but computational efficiency comparisons with other state-of-the-art methods are not standardized (e.g., FLOPs or inference time per image).
